5: A direct revelation: this is my beloved Son with whom I am well pleased; listen to him. The Transfiguration is the unveiling of Christ's eternal glory that the incarnation normally conceals. Moses (the Law) and Elijah (the Prophets) appear as witnesses and then depart; Jesus alone remains. The Father's command listen to him (echoing Deuteronomy 18:15) declares that the entire OT revelation reaches its consummation in the Son — not that Moses and Elijah are superseded as false but that they were preparatory and are now fulfilled. The disciples are to hear the Torah and the Prophets as they hear Jesus, because he is what they were pointing toward.
1: A direct revelation: "And after six days Jesus took with him Peter and James, and John his brother, and led them up a high mountain by themselves." The six-day interval connects the Transfiguration to the Caesarea Philippi confession-and-passion-prediction sequence. The three disciples taken up the mountain are the inner circle who will also be taken to Gethsemane (Matthew 26:37). The high mountain echoes Sinai (Exodus 24:1, where Moses took Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu up the mountain to see God). Jesus is leading his disciples into a theophanic encounter that mirrors and surpasses Sinai.
2: A direct revelation: "And he was transfigured before them, and his face shone like the sun, and his clothes became white as light." The transfiguration (metemorphōthē) is the disclosure of the divine glory that the Son carries within his human form. The shining face recalls Moses' face after the divine encounter (Exodus 34:29-35), but Jesus' glory shines from within — he is not reflecting divine glory but radiating his own. The white-as-light clothing echoes the angel-at-the-tomb description (Matthew 28:3) and the heavenly figures of Daniel 7:9 and Matthew 10:6. This is the pre-resurrection glory of the Son.
3: A direct revelation: "And behold, there appeared to them Moses and Elijah, talking with him." Moses and Elijah — the Law and the Prophets — appear at the Transfiguration as the representatives of the entire OT witness to Jesus. Moses (the giver of Torah who spoke with YHWH face-to-face) and Elijah (the great prophet who was taken up into heaven) flank the Son, their presence declaring: everything they represent finds its fulfillment here. Luke 9:31 reveals the subject of their conversation: his "departure" (exodus), the death-and-resurrection he is about to accomplish in Jerusalem.
4: A revelation of God: "And Peter said to Jesus, 'Lord, it is good that we are here. If you wish, I will make three tents here, one for you and one for Moses and one for Elijah.'" Peter's proposal to build three booths (sukkot — the wilderness dwelling structures of the Feast of Booths, Leviticus 23:42-43) is the attempt to institutionalize the theophanic moment, to dwell in the glory permanently. The Christological problem: three tents place Moses, Elijah, and Jesus on equal footing. The Father's voice (Matthew 17:5) corrects this immediately. Peter's impulse is right (to remain in the presence) but the theology is wrong (the three are not equals).
6: A direct revelation: "When the disciples heard this, they fell on their faces and were terrified." The prostration before the divine voice is the standard OT theophanic response: falling on the face before the overwhelming divine presence (Genesis 17:3; Ezekiel 1:28; Daniel 8:17; Matthew 10:9). The terror (ephobēthēsan sphodra) is the correct response to the holy — not comfort but the awe that recognizes it has encountered something beyond human categories. The disciples have not fallen before an angel or a prophet but before the voice of the living God identifying his Son.
7: A direct revelation: "But Jesus came and touched them, saying, 'Rise, and have no fear.'" The touch of Jesus that dissolves terror is the mediatorial action of the one who bridges the divine and human. The same voice that commands "this is my beloved Son" (the divine declaration of identity) becomes the hand that touches the prostrate disciples. "Rise and have no fear" (egerthēte kai mē phobeisthe) is the resurrection-word (egerthēte) before the resurrection — the same word Jesus will use after Easter (Matthew 28:6: ēgerthē). The Transfiguration is the pre-resurrection disclosure of what the resurrection will reveal.
8: A direct revelation: "And when they lifted up their eyes, they saw no one but Jesus only." The climax of the Transfiguration is the simplest Christological statement: Moses and Elijah have gone; the voice has spoken and fallen silent; and the disciples see "no one but Jesus only" (Iēsoun monon). The Law and the Prophets have been absorbed into him; the divine voice has identified him; and the disciples are left with Christ alone. The Christological summation: at the end of every theophany, every divine encounter, every revelation — only Jesus remains. He is the sum and content of all that God has to say.
9: A direct revelation: "And as they were coming down the mountain, Jesus commanded them, 'Tell no one the vision, until the Son of Man is raised from the dead.'" The seal of silence until the resurrection is the Christological economy of disclosure: the Transfiguration's meaning cannot be properly understood until the resurrection completes it. The glory seen on the mountain is the preview of the resurrection glory; without the cross and empty tomb, the Transfiguration would be misread as a triumph without suffering. "The Son of Man is raised from the dead" is stated here as the interpretive key to everything the disciples have just seen.
10: A direct revelation: "And the disciples asked him, 'Then why do the scribes say that first Elijah must come?'" The disciples' question about Elijah — prompted by seeing Elijah on the mountain — is a Christological query about the order of eschatological events. Malachi 4:5-6 promises that Elijah will come before the great and dreadful day of the LORD. If Jesus is the Messiah, where is Elijah? The question reveals that the disciples are beginning to connect the prophetic pattern to the present moment — they are doing eschatological theology in real time.
11: A direct revelation: "He answered, 'Elijah does come, and he will restore all things.'" Jesus affirms the Elijah tradition before applying it to John the Baptist. The "restoration of all things" (apokatastēsei panta) is the cosmic renewal promised in the prophets — what Acts 3:21 calls the "restoration of all things that God spoke by the mouth of his holy prophets." The Christological frame: the coming of Elijah (John) inaugurates the restoration sequence, and Jesus is the one who will accomplish it. John prepared the way; Jesus will restore all things through the cross and resurrection.
12: A direct revelation: "But I tell you that Elijah has already come, and they did not recognize him, but did to him whatever they pleased. So also the Son of Man will suffer at their hands." The identification of John as the already-come Elijah is paired with the passion prediction for Jesus. The pattern is explicit: the forerunner came and was killed; the Son of Man will follow the same path. The Christological parallel: John's rejection and death prefigures Jesus' rejection and death. The same powers that silenced the herald will silence the proclaimed one — but only for three days.
13: A revelation of God: "Then the disciples understood that he was speaking to them of John the Baptist." The disciples' understanding — explicit and confirmed — is the progressive comprehension that follows each revelation. They now have the hermeneutical key: John = Elijah, Jesus = Son of Man, rejection of John = preview of rejection of Jesus. The Christological education continues: the disciples are being formed to understand the pattern of rejection-then-vindication that governs all of redemptive history.
14: A revelation of God: "And when they came to the crowd, a man came up to him and, kneeling before him, said, 'Lord, have mercy on my son, for he has seizures and he suffers terribly. For often he falls into the fire, and often into the water.'" The descent from the Transfiguration to the valley of demonic torment is the Christological movement of the incarnation in miniature: from divine glory into the suffering of the world. The kneeling father's petition ("Lord, have mercy") is the prayer of the Psalms directed to the one whose glory has just been revealed. From the mountain of transfiguration to the valley of suffering — this is where the Lord's mercy operates.
15: A revelation of God: "And I brought him to your disciples, and they could not heal him." The disciples' failure to heal the epileptic boy is the Christological test of their received authority (Matthew 10:1: Jesus gave them authority over unclean spirits). The failure is not a permanent limitation but the occasion for instruction. The Christological bearing: the disciples' inadequacy is the space in which Jesus' sufficiency is revealed — and the occasion for teaching about the faith that accesses that sufficiency (Matthew 17:20).
16: A revelation of God: "And I brought him to your disciples, and they could not heal him." (See Matthew 17:15 — this verse continues the father's report.) The juxtaposition of the disciples' failure with Jesus' immediate healing (Matthew 17:18) is the Christological contrast between delegated authority poorly appropriated and the original authority of the Son. The disciples have the authority but not yet the faith to exercise it consistently. The teacher's authority and the disciples' authority are the same kind but not the same degree.
17: A direct revelation: "And Jesus answered, 'O faithless and twisted generation, how long am I to be with you? How long am I to bear with you? Bring him here to me.'" The cry "how long am I to be with you?" is the Christological statement of the Incarnation's temporariness: Jesus knows his time is limited, and the disciples' continued failure to exercise the faith entrusted to them is a burden to him. The "faithless and twisted generation" echoes Moses' lament at Israel's unbelief in the wilderness (Deuteronomy 32:5, 20). Jesus is the new Moses bearing with a generation that will not fully believe.
18: A direct revelation: "And Jesus rebuked the demon, and it came out of him, and the boy was healed instantly." The immediate healing — in contrast to the disciples' failed attempt — is the Christological demonstration of the authority that the disciples are called to exercise. The rebuke of the demon is the authority of the Son of God (Matthew 8:29: the demons recognized Jesus as "Son of God"). The instant healing is the signature of his presence: "as many as touched him were healed" (Matthew 14:36). His authority is not effortful but inherent.
19: A revelation of God: "Then the disciples came to Jesus privately and said, 'Why could we not cast it out?'" The private question — asked in the pattern of the parable-explanations (Matthew 13:10, 36) — is the disciples' honest acknowledgment of failure and desire to understand. The Christological pedagogy: the disciples who fail and then ask are the disciples who learn. The failure, the question, and the private teaching are the threefold pattern of Matthean discipleship formation.
20: A direct revelation: "He said to them, 'Because of your little faith. For truly, I say to you, if you have faith like a grain of mustard seed, you will say to this mountain, 'Move from here to there,' and it will move, and nothing will be impossible for you.'" The mustard seed of faith (the smallest seed, Matthew 13:31) that moves mountains is the paradox of kingdom power: not the size of faith but its object. Faith in the one who has all authority can do what the authority-holder can do — and "nothing will be impossible." The Christological basis: faith that is directed to the Son of God, who holds all authority, participates in his omnipotence. The disciples' failure was not insufficient faith but misdirected or insufficient trust in the one who authorized them.
21: A revelation of God: (Matthew 17:21 absent in many manuscripts — "this kind does not come out except by prayer and fasting" — but present in some tradition.) Even in the textual tradition that includes this verse, the principle is Christological: the deep work of spiritual warfare requires the dependence on God that prayer and fasting cultivate. The disciples' independence from the Father in their attempt to heal is the root of their failure; dependence is the posture of kingdom authority.
22: A direct revelation: "As they were gathering in Galilee, Jesus said to them, 'The Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men.'" The second passion prediction introduces the theme of being "delivered" (paradidosthai) — the same verb used for Judas's betrayal (Matthew 26:25) and for the handing-over to the Gentiles (Matthew 27:2). The passive implies divine agency: the Son of Man is "delivered" according to the Father's plan. The gathering in Galilee before the journey to Jerusalem gives the passion prediction its geographical urgency.
23: A direct revelation: "And they will kill him, and he will be raised on the third day." And they were greatly distressed." The passion prediction ends with the resurrection as its inseparable counterpart — kill and raise, death and third-day vindication. The disciples' distress (elypēthēsan sphodra) shows they hear the death but do not yet comprehend the resurrection. The Christological pattern: the passion predictions consistently pair suffering with resurrection, but the disciples consistently hear only the suffering. Understanding the resurrection is the hermeneutical key to all that follows.
24: A revelation of God: "When they came to Capernaum, the collectors of the two-drachma tax came to Peter and said, 'Does your teacher not pay the tax?'" The temple tax question — the half-shekel assessment required of every Jewish male (Exodus 30:13-16) — creates the occasion for Jesus' statement about sonship and freedom (Matthew 17:26). The collectors' question about "your teacher" is the respectful but slightly distancing address of those who are not disciples. The answer Jesus gives will reveal his unique relationship to the Temple and its Lord.
25: A direct revelation: "He said, 'Yes.' And when he came into the house, Jesus spoke to him first, saying, 'What do you think, Simon? From whom do kings of the earth take toll or tax? From their sons or from others?'" Jesus' question to Peter — before Peter has reported the tax-collector conversation — again shows his knowledge of what has occurred (cf. Matthew 12:25: "knowing their thoughts"). The question about kings' sons is the setup for the Christological claim: the son of the king does not pay the king's tax. Jesus is the Son of the King whose house the temple is.
26: A direct revelation: "And when he said, 'From others,' Jesus said to him, 'Then the sons are free.'" The Christological claim is elegant: if kings' sons are exempt from their father's taxes, then the Son of God is exempt from the temple tax — because the temple is his Father's house (Matthew 21:13: "my house"). The freedom of the Son is the freedom of the one who inhabits the Father's house by right, not by duty. The statement "the sons are free" carries the eschatological weight of the freedom Christ will purchase for those who are adopted as sons through him (Romans 8:15-17).
27: A direct revelation: "However, not to give offense to them, go to the sea and cast a hook and take the first fish that comes up, and when you open its mouth you will find a shekel. Take that and give it to them for me and for yourself." The miraculous provision of the tax-coin from the fish's mouth is the Christological combination of the freedom of the Son with the grace of the servant: he is free from the obligation but pays it voluntarily to avoid giving offense. The fish that provides the coin anticipates the feeding miracles' pattern — provision through ordinary means (fishing) by extraordinary divine knowledge and control. He pays his own tax from the abundance of creation.
